title: The One With the Race Car Bed

expressions.expression: go in one ear and out the other
expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "So I told Carl, 'Nobody, no matter how famous their parents are, nobody is allowed to climb on the dinosaur.' But of course **this went in one ear and out**....."
Rachel: (thinking) "I love how he cares so much about stuff. If I squint I can pretend he's Alan Alda."
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 말을 듣고도 금방 잊어버리거나 전혀 신경 쓰지 않음
expressions.nuance: 누군가가 내 말을 흘려들을 때 쓰는 고정 표현. "goes in one ear and out the other"가 완전형이지만 Ross처럼 "out" 앞에서 잘라내도 청자가 즉각 이해함. 체념이나 답답함의 감정이 담김.
expressions.example: I've told my roommate to clean the kitchen a dozen times, but it just goes in one ear and out the other. / 룸메이트한테 부엌 청소하라고 열두 번은 얘기했는데 그냥 흘려들어.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "**Okay, some tricks of the trade**. Now, I've never been able to cry as an actor, so if I'm in a scene where I have to cry, I cut a hole in my pocket, take a pair of tweezers, and just start pulling."
Joey: "Or ah, let's say I wanna convey that I've just done something evil. That would be the basic 'I have a fishhook in my eyebrow and I like it.'"
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 특정 직업이나 분야에서 현장 경험으로 터득한 실용적인 비법이나 노하우
expressions.nuance: 공식 교육이 아닌 insider가 공유하는 현장 팁. "tips"보다 더 전문적이고 내부자적인 뉘앙스. 가르치거나 비결을 전수할 때 자연스럽게 씀.
expressions.example: After ten years in the restaurant business, she's learned all the tricks of the trade. / 식당에서 10년을 일하고 나니 그녀는 온갖 비법을 다 익혔어.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "**I got a gig**!"
All: "Yay!!"
Chandler: "See, that's why I could never be an actor. Because I can't say gig."
Phoebe: "Yeah, I can't say croissant. Oh my God!"
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: (비공식) 일거리, 공연, 단기 직업 — 특히 예술·프리랜서 분야
expressions.nuance: 정규 job보다 단발성이거나 비정기적인 일에 씀. 배우·음악가·프리랜서가 자주 씀. "I got a job"보다 더 들뜨고 캐주얼한 느낌. Chandler의 농담처럼 발음 자체가 경쾌하고 배우스러운 단어.
expressions.example: She picked up a gig singing at weddings on weekends to make extra cash. / 그녀는 주말에 결혼식 노래 공연을 잡아서 부수입을 벌었어.

occ.speaker: Susan
occ.text: Oh, that's so... (Susan hugs Carol, they giggle, Ross steps away) It really is...do we know...?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: But, but this is my gig. This is where I play. My, my name is written out there in chalk. You know, you can't just erase chalk.

occ.speaker: Dr. Burke
occ.text: Hi Phoebe, nice to meet you. [Phoebe just giggles when they shake] So, how ya been?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "What's the part?"
Joey: "Well, it's not a part, no. I'm teaching acting for soap operas down at the Learning Extension."
Ross: "Come on! That's great."
Joey: "Yeah, yeah. It's like my chance to **give something back to** the acting community."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 자신이 받은 것에 대한 보답으로 어떤 집단이나 사회에 기여하다, 환원하다
expressions.nuance: 단순 봉사보다 감사와 의무감이 섞인 표현. "contribute to"보다 더 개인적이고 감정적인 연결이 느껴짐. 성공한 사람이 업계나 지역사회에 돌려준다는 맥락에서 자주 씀.
expressions.example: After years of support from fans, she started a scholarship fund to give something back to her community. / 오랜 팬들의 응원에 보답하고자 그녀는 지역 장학 기금을 만들었어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Well, I think it's great that the medical community is finally trying to help sick hamsters.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Well, the gay community is a lot more vocal than the dead community.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "Okay, look, here's the bottom line Ross, this is fixable, if we act fast, okay. So, I'll invite him to brunch tomorrow and you can **make nice**."
Ross: "Look, honey, I have tried to **make nice**, it doesn't work."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: (갈등 이후) 사이좋게 지내려 노력하다, 친하게 지내는 척하다
expressions.nuance: 진심 어린 화해보다 전략적·의도적인 친절에 가까운 뉘앙스. "be nice"보다 능동적이고 목적이 있음. 상황 관리 차원에서 억지로 친절한 태도를 취할 때도 씀.
expressions.example: Even if you two don't get along, just make nice for the sake of the dinner. / 두 사람 사이가 안 좋더라도 저녁 식사를 위해 그냥 친하게 지내는 척해봐.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Look, you have to help me! Okay? I mean, I know what to do with a woman, y'know, I know where everything goes, it's always nice. But I need to know what makes it go from nice to, "My God! Somebody's killing her in there!"

occ.speaker: Janine
occ.text: Well, I'm sorry. I just thought I'd try to make the place a little nicer.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: How about you go put on your 007 tuxedo and I'll make you a nice martini.

expressions.expression: be the bigger man / be the bigger person
expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "Okay, look, Ross, I realise that my Father is difficult, but that's why you have got to **be the bigger man** here."
Ross: "Look sweetie, I could be the bigger man, I could be the biggest man, I could be a big, huge, giant man, and it still wouldn't make any difference..."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 갈등 상황에서 먼저 양보하거나 자존심을 내려놓고 성숙하게 대처하다
expressions.nuance: 상대방이 잘못했더라도 내가 먼저 한발 물러서는 것. 도덕적 우위를 강조하는 표현. 성별 중립적으로 "bigger person"도 씀. Ross의 코믹한 반응처럼 과장해서 비틀 수도 있음.
expressions.example: He said some awful things, but I decided to be the bigger man and apologize first. / 그가 심한 말을 했지만 나는 더 성숙하게 먼저 사과하기로 했어.

expressions.expression: face it / you gotta face it
expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "Okay, well can't you just try it one more time Ross? For me?"
Ross: "Rachel one brunch is not gonna solve anything. **You gotta face it**, okay, we're never gonna get along."
Rachel: "Okay, well you are just gonna have to, okay, because I already got a Mother and a Father who cannot stay in the same room together..."
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 불편한 현실을 인정하다, 직시하다
expressions.nuance: "Let's face it" 또는 "You gotta face it"처럼 앞에 붙어서 씀. 상대방에게 혹은 자기 자신에게 현실을 직시하라고 촉구할 때의 뉘앙스. 약간 체념이나 단호함이 섞임.
expressions.example: Face it, she's not coming back. You need to move on. / 현실을 봐, 그녀는 돌아오지 않아. 이제 앞으로 나아가야 해.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: And everyone's telling me, you gotta pick a major, you gotta pick a major. So, on a dare, I picked paleontology. And you have no idea what I'm saying, because, let's face it, you're a fetus. You're just happy you don't have gills anymore.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I wish we at least knew his name... Look at that face. I mean, even sleeping, he looks smart. I bet he's a lawyer.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I'm telling you, that monkey is a chick magnet! She's going to take one look at his furry, cute little face and it'll seal the deal.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "You had to do it, didn't you? **You couldn't just leave it alone**."
Ross: "Four percent. Okay. I tip more than that when there's a bug in my food."
Rachel: "Ross, tonight was about the two of you getting along."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 건드리지 않고 그냥 내버려두다 (상황을 악화시키지 말라는 뜻)
expressions.nuance: 상황이 잘 정리되고 있는데 굳이 끼어들어 긁어 부스럼을 만드는 사람에게 쓰는 표현. "let it go"와 비슷하지만 더 직접적이고 답답함이 담김.
expressions.example: The argument was dying down, but he couldn't leave it alone and made everything worse. / 말싸움이 잦아들고 있었는데 그가 가만두지 않아서 상황을 더 악화시켰어.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Y'know what?  I think we can just leave it at that.  It's kinda like a symbolic gesture...

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Yeah, yeah, it's interesting.. but y'know what? Just for fun, let's see what it looked like in the old spot. (She moves it.) Alright, just to compare. Let's see. Well, it looks good there too. Let's just leave it there for a while.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I can't leave it! You gouged a hole in my dingy floor.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: "You told him to play the boxer gay!!"
Joey: "Well, I-I might've said supergay."
Chandler: "**You totally screwed him over**."
Monica: "Joey, you're this guy's teacher. I mean how could you do this?"
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 누군가를 배신하거나 이용해 손해를 끼치다
expressions.nuance: 비속어에 가깝지만 일상 대화에서 매우 흔히 씀. "betray"보다 구어적이고 결과적 피해를 강조함. "totally"처럼 부사와 결합해 강도를 높이는 경우가 많음.
expressions.example: My business partner screwed me over by selling his shares without telling me. / 사업 파트너가 나한테 말도 없이 지분을 팔아버려 배신당했어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: You know, when you start get screwed over all the time, you gotta switch to low-fat.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Okay. (singing) 'Jingle bitch screwed me over! Go to hell jingle whore! Go to hell Go to hell. Go to hell-hell-hell.' That's all I have so far.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: You obviously haven't screwed over a lot of your friends. (They all look at her) Which we all appreciate.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: "Hey, I'm going for sushi does anybody want.. (enters and sees the bed) **Whoa-whoa, somebody missed the off ramp**."
Phoebe: "It's Monica's bed. What?"
Chandler: "Okay. It's a racecar."
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: (비유) 방향을 잘못 잡았다, 완전히 엉뚱한 곳에 와 있다
expressions.nuance: 고속도로 출구(off ramp)를 놓쳤다는 뜻에서 유래. 레이스카 침대가 침실에 있는 황당한 상황을 유머러스하게 표현한 것. 어떤 선택이나 상황이 크게 어긋났을 때 농담처럼 씀.
expressions.example: A ball pit in a corporate office? Looks like someone missed the off ramp. / 회사 사무실에 볼 풀이라고? 완전 방향을 잘못 잡은 것 같은데.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "I advised a fellow actor to play a role, homosexually. Yeah, we both auditioned for the part, and uh, **as it turned out**, they liked the stupid gay thing and cast him."
Joey: "And now, he's got a two year contract opposite Susan Luchhi, the first lady of daytime television..."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 결국 알고 보니, 나중에 밝혀보니 — 예상과 다른 결과를 이야기할 때
expressions.nuance: 이야기 흐름에서 결말이나 반전을 부드럽게 도입하는 표현. 계획이나 예상과 달랐을 때 자주 씀. "It turned out that..."보다 문장 앞에 붙는 형태로 자연스러움.
expressions.example: I was worried the pitch would flop, but as it turned out, the client loved every idea. / 발표가 망할까봐 걱정했는데, 알고 보니 클라이언트가 모든 아이디어를 좋아했어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (as Rachel) Um, okay. You just called a little while ago about needing a signature on the admissions form. Well, it turns out we need a whole new one (little laugh) because uh, you see, I-I, I put the wrong name again. (Little laugh) 'cause um...

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I'm at work, ordinary day, you know, chop chop chop, sauti, sauti, sauti. All of a sudden, Leon, the manager, calls me into his office. It turns out they fired the head lunch chef, and guess who got the job.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Becasue at first he thought it was Joseph. But after he asked Joseph about it, turns out it was you. Anyway, I just thought you should know.

expressions.expression: be stuck doing something / be stuck here
expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "And now, he's got a two year contract opposite Susan Luchhi, the first lady of daytime television, and me, me **I'm stuck here** teaching a bunch of people, most of whom are too ugly to even be on TV. I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry."
(he gets a huge round of applause from his students.)
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 원치 않는 상황에 갇혀 어쩔 수 없이 ~하다
expressions.nuance: 선택의 여지 없이 하기 싫은 일을 해야 할 때 씀. "stuck here" 뒤에 장소나 동명사가 오거나 "stuck doing X" 형태로 씀. Joey의 자조적 한탄처럼 감정적 무게가 실림.
expressions.example: I'm stuck doing weekend shifts until they hire someone new. / 새 직원이 생길 때까지 어쩔 수 없이 주말 근무를 해야 해.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Listen uh, I'm really sorry, it looks like I'm gonna be stuck here for a while. I got the transmission fluid, but when I went to put it in the car, the transmission wasn't there!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: No, it's not that, I just don't want to be stuck here all night with your fat sister.

expressions.expression: That's it, I can't take it anymore
expressions.dialogue:
Dr. Green: "Nice hair. What'd ya do? Swim here?"
Ross: (to Rachel) "**Okay, that's it, I can't take it anymore**."
Rachel: "What? What? He's interested in you. He likes your hair, he just wants to know how you got here."
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 더 이상 못 참겠다, 인내의 한계에 달했다
expressions.nuance: 오랜 참음 끝에 폭발하기 직전에 쓰는 표현. "That's it!" 단독으로도 씀. 뒤에 행동 선언이 따라올 때가 많음. "I've had it", "I've had enough"와 비슷한 뉘앙스.
expressions.example: That's it, I can't take it anymore. I'm calling the landlord right now. / 됐어, 이제 못 참겠어. 지금 당장 집주인한테 전화할 거야.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: "Oh, hey, hey Nick the boxer let's see what you got. All right ya, **put 'em up**. All right. Come on."
(they start shadow boxing)
Joey: "Hey, you're ah, pretty good at this."
Phoebe: "Yeah, well I had to learn, I was staying at the Y and some of the young men weren't acting Christian enough."
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 주먹을 들어라 — 싸움이나 복싱 자세를 요구하는 표현
expressions.nuance: 복싱에서 유래. 실제 대결뿐 아니라 장난스럽게 도전하거나 농담으로 "한번 붙어보자"고 할 때도 씀. 'em = them (your fists).
expressions.example: Oh yeah? You think you can beat me at arm wrestling? Put 'em up! / 그래? 팔씨름으로 날 이길 수 있다고? 어디 한번 해봐!

expressions.dialogue:
Dr. Green: "This is nice. I pay two hundred dollars for dinner, you put down twenty, and you come out looking like **Mr. Big Shot**. You really want to be **Mr. Big Shot**? Here, I'll tell you what, you pay the whole bill, **Mr. Big Shot**, all right."
Ross: "Well Mr. Big Shot is better than 'wethead'."
expressions.speaker: Dr. Green
expressions.meaning: 스스로 대단하거나 중요한 사람인 척하는 사람 — 으스대는 사람을 조롱하는 표현
expressions.nuance: 실제 자격 없이 과시하거나 허세를 부리는 사람에게 씀. 비꼬는 뉘앙스가 강함. "acting like a big shot"도 자주 씀. Dr. Green이 반복해서 쓰며 조롱의 강도를 높이는 것이 포인트.
expressions.example: He got one promotion and now he's strutting around like Mr. Big Shot. / 승진 한 번 했다고 이제 잘난 척하면서 돌아다니네.

expressions.dialogue:
Mattress King: (on TV) "I'm so depressed I'm going to slash... my prices!! Check it out! Four ninety-nine for a pillow top queen set! **I'm going medieval on prices**!"
Chandler: "What a wank!"
Janice: "Oh, I cannot believe he's using our divorce to sell mattresses."
expressions.speaker: Mattress King (TV)
expressions.meaning: 무언가에 극단적이거나 거칠고 과격하게 대처하다
expressions.nuance: Pulp Fiction(1994)의 유명 대사에서 유래해 1990년대 미국에서 유행한 표현. 강력하고 거침없이 행동한다는 과장된 선언. 현재도 유머러스하게 씀.
expressions.example: After the audit, the CFO went medieval on every department's expenses. / 감사 이후 CFO는 모든 부서 경비를 가차없이 들여다봤어.

expressions.dialogue:
Mattress King: (on TV) "I'm so depressed I'm going to slash... my prices!! **Check it out!** Four ninety-nine for a pillow top queen set! I'm going medieval on prices!"
Chandler: "What a wank!"
Janice: "Oh, I cannot believe he's using our divorce to sell mattresses."
expressions.speaker: Mattress King (TV)
expressions.meaning: 이것 좀 봐봐, 한번 봐 — 주목할 것을 가리킬 때 쓰는 표현
expressions.nuance: 매우 캐주얼한 표현. 뭔가 흥미롭거나 놀라운 것을 보여주거나 알릴 때 앞에 붙임. 광고에서처럼 흥분이나 기대를 조성하는 데도 씀.
expressions.example: Check it out, they're giving out free samples over there! / 저기 봐봐, 무료 샘플 나눠주고 있어!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (to Ross) Hey! Here's the birthday boy! Ross, check it out: hockey tickets, Rangers-Penguins, tonight at the Garden, and we're taking you.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Check it out, Giants-Cowboys. (He has a pocket TV)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Check it out, check it out. Heckles' high school yearbook.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "And now, he's got a two year contract opposite Susan Luchhi, the first lady of daytime television, and me, me I'm stuck here teaching **a bunch of people**, most of whom are too ugly to even be on TV. I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry."
(he gets a huge round of applause from his students.)
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 많은, 한 무더기의 — 사람이나 사물을 비공식적으로 가리키는 수량 표현
expressions.nuance: "a lot of"보다 더 캐주얼하고 약간 무관심하거나 폄하하는 뉘앙스가 있을 수 있음. 사람에게 쓰면 '그냥 많은 무리'라는 느낌. Joey처럼 자조적인 맥락에서는 가벼운 경멸감도 담김.
expressions.example: There was a bunch of people waiting outside when the doors opened. / 문이 열렸을 때 밖에 사람들이 잔뜩 기다리고 있었어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Just breathe, breathe.. that's it. Just try to think of nice calm things...

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Kinda puts that whole pillow thing in perspective, huh, Mon?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Don't think of it as a cigarette. Think of it as the thing that's been missing from your hand. When you're holding it, you feel right. You feel complete.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "Ross, my father doesn't hate you."
Ross: "Please, he **refers to me as** 'wethead'."
Rachel: "But honey he calls everybody by a nickname!"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~를 ~라고 부르다, ~라는 이름/호칭으로 지칭하다
expressions.nuance: "call"보다 일관되고 의도적인 호칭 사용을 강조할 때 씀. 공식 호칭이 아닌 별명이나 특이한 호칭을 언급할 때 자주 씀. 다소 격식을 차리거나 거리감을 두는 뉘앙스도 있음.
expressions.example: My boss refers to all the interns as 'the kids,' which they absolutely hate. / 우리 매니저는 인턴들을 전부 '꼬마들'이라고 부르는데 그들은 정말 싫어해.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Well, wait a minute, wha- why is she in the title?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: The exclamation point in the title scares me. (Gesturing) Y'know, it's not just Freud, it's Freud!

occ.speaker: Susan
occ.text: But um, we just refer to you as Bobo the Sperm Guy.

expressions.dialogue:
Dr. Green: "So, why do you **let her go** to a chiropractor for?"
Rachel: "I'm sorry, **let** her?"
Ross: "What can I do, she doesn't listen to me about renter's insurance either."
expressions.speaker: Dr. Green / Rachel
expressions.meaning: 누군가가 ~하도록 허락하다 / 내버려두다
expressions.nuance: "let"은 원래 허락의 의미지만, 상대방이 어른인데 쓰면 그 사람의 자율성을 무시하는 가부장적 전제를 담게 됨. Rachel이 즉각 "let her?"라고 반박하는 것이 이 단어의 권력 뉘앙스를 정확히 꼬집는 장면.
expressions.example: "Why do you let her stay out so late?" "Excuse me, let her? She's a grown adult." / "왜 그렇게 늦게까지 있게 두는 거야?" "잠깐, '있게 둔다'고? 그녀는 어른이야."

occ.speaker: Lizzie
occ.text: Please, let me do something.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: The hell with hockey, let's all do that!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (to the crowd in the laundromat) All right, show's over. Nothing to see here. (to Rachel) Ok, let's do laundry.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "And four ninety-nine for a pillow top queen set, **who cares about the divorce**, those babies will sell themselves."
(they all stare at her)
Monica: "And I'm appalled for you by the way."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: ~가 무슨 상관이야, ~가 뭐가 중요해
expressions.nuance: 어떤 것에 신경 쓸 필요가 없다고 단호하게 말할 때 씀. 때론 진심이 아닌 유머나 솔직한 우선순위 전환으로 씀. Monica가 가격에 흥분해 이혼 스캔들을 잠시 잊어버리는 장면이 이 표현의 캐릭터를 잘 보여줌.
expressions.example: Who cares about the weather? We're going to the beach either way! / 날씨가 뭐가 중요해? 어차피 해변에 갈 거잖아!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Who cares, we'll eat at the sink! Come on, let's play!

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: All right, fine. Um, you were not the only one there. (Camera fades to Ross, who's listening very carefully) Joey was there too.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: No, no, no... why, because it might get weird for everyone else? Who cares about them. This is about us. Look, I-I've been in love with you since, like, the ninth grade.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "And four ninety-nine for a pillow top queen set, who cares about the divorce, **those babies will sell themselves**."
(they all stare at her)
Monica: "And I'm appalled for you by the way."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 광고나 홍보 없이도 품질이 좋아 저절로 팔릴 것이다
expressions.nuance: "babies"는 여기서 제품을 친근하게 부르는 표현. "sell itself"는 좋은 제품·아이디어가 스스로를 증명한다는 의미. 마케팅·비즈니스 맥락에서 자주 씀.
expressions.example: A product this good basically sells itself — you barely need to market it. / 이 정도 제품이면 따로 마케팅할 필요도 없이 알아서 팔려.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Noooo. No-no-no, these babies sell themselves.

expressions.expression: go over someone's head / went right over your head
expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: (to a kid at the race car bed display) "Hi. Y'know in England this car would be on the other side of the store."
[The kid just stares at her blankly; Phoebe makes the '**that went right over your head**' gesture and says 'Woo!']
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: (말·농담·개념이) 상대방이 이해하지 못하다, 통하지 않다
expressions.nuance: 농담이나 설명이 상대방의 이해 수준을 넘어서서 전혀 통하지 않을 때 씀. "that went over your head"처럼 과거형으로 사후에 쓰거나, "this might go over their head"처럼 예측할 때도 씀. 당황스럽지만 유머러스하게 표현 가능.
expressions.example: I made a subtle joke about the company's Q3 losses, but it went completely over his head. / 회사 3분기 적자에 관한 은근한 농담을 했는데 그 사람한테는 전혀 통하지 않았어.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "Hey, would you ah, would you like some juice?"
Dr. Green: "**I'd love some juice**. Thanks."
Ross: "Okay." (to Rachel) "Wow! This is going so well. Did you see us? Did you see?"
expressions.speaker: Dr. Green
expressions.meaning: ~을 정말 갖고 싶다, ~을 기꺼이 받겠다 — 제안에 대한 열의 있는 수락
expressions.nuance: "Yes, please"보다 더 열정적이고 감사함이 담긴 수락 표현. "would love"는 공손하면서도 진심 어린 원함을 나타냄. 음식·음료 제안에 특히 자연스럽게 씀.
expressions.example: "Can I get you something to drink?" "I'd love some coffee, thank you." / "뭔가 마실 거 드릴까요?" "커피를 마시면 정말 좋겠어요, 감사합니다."

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Okay. Okay, I got one. Do you remember that vegetarian pate that I made that you loved so much?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh, I would love some. But y'know what? Y'know what? Let's not drink it here. I'm feeling kinda crazy. You wanna go to Newark?

occ.speaker: Barry
occ.text: I'm sorry... I'm sorry, God, I am so sorry, I'm an idiot, I was weak, I couldn't help myself! Whatever I did, I only did because I love you so much!

expressions.dialogue:
Student: "I was wondering if you would consider coaching me for it?"
Joey: "**You bet!** What's the part?"
Student: "Oh it's great, it's a role on All My Children, Nick the boxer."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 물론이지!, 당연하죠! — 긍정·동의·확신을 나타내는 표현
expressions.nuance: "Sure!" 또는 "Absolutely!"와 비슷하지만 더 미국적이고 유쾌하며 즉각적인 뉘앙스. 누군가의 부탁이나 제안에 기꺼이 응할 때 씀. 대답이면서 동시에 상대방을 응원하는 느낌도 담김.
expressions.example: "Would you be willing to help with the event?" "You bet! Just tell me what you need." / "행사 도와줄 수 있어요?" "물론이죠! 뭐가 필요한지 말해줘요."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh God Monica hi! Thank God! I just went to your building and you weren't there and then this guy with a big hammer said you might be here and you are, you are!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Yeah. Do that for another two hours, you might be where I am right about now. (He enters.)

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, but if you spent it, it would be like shopping!
